stimulus: The body plan of brittle stars (class Ophiuroidea) — slender, highly flexible arms radiating from a small central disk — complicates straightforward comparisons with most other animals, whose anatomies are organized along a bilateral head-to-tail axis. Particularly puzzling is the location of nervous-system structures: in most bilateral animals these are concentrated in a discrete head, but brittle stars are descended from bilateral ancestors yet appear headless. Mapping the expression of well-characterized head-, trunk-, and tail-region patterning genes from a closely related bilateral relative onto the brittle star Amphiura filiformis, researchers found strong activity of head-associated genes throughout each arm and only weak, peripheral activity of trunk- and tail-associated genes, suggesting that ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: rather than losing a head as their radial body plan developed, brittle stars evolved a body in which head-like genetic identity is distributed across all of the arms.
although they appear superficially radial, brittle stars are best understood as bilateral animals whose head and tail regions are simply small and difficult to detect.
despite their close relationship to other invertebrates, brittle stars share almost none of the body-patterning genes that are typical of bilateral animals.
the strong expression of trunk-associated genes at the edges of the arms is primarily responsible for the brittle star's characteristic radial symmetry.

rationale:
Choice A is the best answer. The researchers observed head-region gene activity throughout the arms and only weak, peripheral trunk/tail activity. The most logical inference is that brittle stars did not simply discard a head when they became radial; instead, head-like genetic identity is spread across the whole body, with arms behaving as extensions of "head" rather than a separate trunk plus tail.

Choice B is incorrect because the data show widely distributed head-region activity, not small, hidden head and tail regions. Choice C is incorrect because the researchers were able to map head, trunk, and tail genes onto the brittle star — implying the genes are present, just expressed in a different pattern. Choice D is incorrect because trunk gene activity at the edges was described as weak/limited, so it cannot be the primary driver of overall radial symmetry.
